response: The business-research problem is how a warehouse should jointly batch and route the orders known at a cutoff time to reduce total picker travel distance. Given released orders, item locations, and cart capacity, the integer program assigns every cutoff order to one batch and constructs continuous picker routes, subject to cart capacity, with total distance as the objective. The computational difficulty comes from the interaction between batching and routing: batch composition determines which item locations can be visited together, which in turn changes route length. Reduced walking distance can reasonably be interpreted as lower modeled travel burden, but no stronger operating effect is established. Orders arriving after the cutoff, congestion, and picker learning are outside the model, so the research must not attribute improvements to those mechanisms. Evidence would be needed before translating distance reductions into labor-time, cost, congestion, or learning effects.
